questions.optionB: Massive proteinuria (>3.5g/day), hypoalbuminemia, generalized edema, hyperlipidemia, and lipiduria
questions.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome: podocyte injury → loss of charge/size barrier → massive proteinuria (>3.5g/day). Hypoalbuminemia → decreased oncotic pressure → edema. Liver compensatory increase in protein synthesis → hyperlipidemia/lipiduria (oval fat bodies, Maltese crosses). Most common cause in children: minimal change disease (MCD, nil disease — responds to steroids). In adults: membranous nephropathy (anti-PLA2R antibodies). Also: FSGS (most common in AA, HIV), diabetic nephropathy, amyloidosis. Reference: Robbins — Nephrotic Syndrome.

questions.questionText: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) typically presents with:
questions.optionB: Episodic gross hematuria 1-2 days after an upper respiratory infection (synpharyngitic)
questions.explanation: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease): most common glomerulonephritis WORLDWIDE. Mesangial IgA deposits. Presents: episodic gross hematuria (painless) occurring within 1-2 DAYS of upper respiratory or GI infection (synpharyngitic — vs. post-streptococcal GN which occurs 1-3 WEEKS after infection). IF: mesangial IgA deposits (granular). EM: mesangial dense deposits. Serum IgA may be elevated. HSP (Henoch-Schönlein purpura): systemic form with IgA vasculitis (skin, joints, GI). Reference: Robbins — IgA Nephropathy.

questions.questionText: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN) is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Crescent formation in glomeruli (crescentic GN) with rapid loss of renal function (days to weeks)
questions.explanation: RPGN: rapid decline in GFR (days-weeks) with crescents on biopsy (proliferating parietal epithelial cells + macrophages in Bowman's space). Three types: (I) Anti-GBM (linear IF — Goodpasture), (II) Immune complex (granular IF — lupus nephritis, IgA, post-infectious), (III) Pauci-immune (negative IF, ANCA+ — granulomatosis with polyangiitis/Wegener's, microscopic polyangiitis). Type III is most common. Emergent treatment: plasmapheresis (Type I), immunosuppression. Reference: Robbins — RPGN.

questions.questionText: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) typically presents 1-3 weeks after:
questions.optionB: Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis or skin infection (impetigo)
questions.explanation: PSGN: immune complex-mediated (Type III hypersensitivity). Occurs 1-3 WEEKS after GAS infection (pharyngitis or impetigo — latent period for immune complex formation). Most common in children (6-10 years). Nephritic syndrome: hematuria (cola-colored urine, RBC casts), hypertension, oliguria, periorbital edema. Labs: low C3 (complement consumption), elevated ASO/anti-DNase B titers. IF: granular ('lumpy-bumpy') IgG/C3 deposition. EM: subepithelial 'humps.' Self-limiting in children. Reference: Robbins — PSGN.

questions.questionText: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) classically presents with:
questions.optionB: Triad of hematuria, flank pain, and palpable abdominal mass (classic triad seen in 10%)
questions.explanation: RCC: most common primary renal malignancy in adults. Clear cell type most common (~70%, VHL gene loss on 3p). Classic triad (only ~10%): hematuria (most common presenting symptom), flank pain, palpable mass. Paraneoplastic: polycythemia (EPO), hypercalcemia (PTHrP), Stauffer syndrome (liver dysfunction without metastasis). Invades renal vein → IVC (tumor thrombus). Golden-yellow tumor (lipid and glycogen in clear cells). Associated with smoking, VHL syndrome, obesity. Reference: Robbins — RCC.

questions.optionB: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules (nodular glomerulosclerosis), thickened GBM, and mesangial expansion
questions.explanation: Diabetic nephropathy: most common cause of end-stage renal disease (ESRD) in developed countries. Progressive stages: hyperfiltration → microalbuminuria → overt proteinuria → nephrotic syndrome → ESRD. Pathology: diffuse mesangial expansion (most common), Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules (nodular glomerulosclerosis — pathognomonic), thickened GBM, arteriolar hyalinosis (afferent AND efferent — unique to diabetes). Treatment: ACEi/ARBs (reduce intraglomerular pressure), SGLT2 inhibitors. Reference: Robbins — Diabetic Nephropathy.

questions.questionText: Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) is a childhood renal tumor associated with:
questions.optionB: WT1/WT2 gene loss, WAGR syndrome, and Denys-Drash syndrome
questions.explanation: Wilms tumor: most common renal malignancy in children (peak 2-5 years). Large, well-circumscribed, encapsulated. Triphasic histology: blastemal + stromal + epithelial. WT1 (chromosome 11p13): tumor suppressor. WAGR syndrome: Wilms, Aniridia, GU anomalies, mental Retardation (WT1 deletion). Denys-Drash: Wilms + intersex + nephropathy (WT1 point mutation). Beckwith-Wiedemann: overgrowth syndrome (WT2, 11p15.5). Unilateral flank mass that does NOT cross midline (vs. neuroblastoma which does). Reference: Robbins — Wilms.

questions.questionText: Minimal change disease (nil disease) on electron microscopy shows:
questions.optionB: Effacement (fusion) of podocyte foot processes with NO immune complex deposits
questions.explanation: MCD: most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in CHILDREN. Light microscopy: normal (nil disease). IF: negative. EM: diffuse effacement (fusion) of podocyte foot processes — characteristic finding. No immune deposits. Selective proteinuria (albumin mostly). Excellent response to corticosteroids (>90%). May be triggered by respiratory infections, NSAIDs, Hodgkin lymphoma. Relapse is common but prognosis is excellent. Not progressive to renal failure. Reference: Robbins — Minimal Change Disease.
